title: Truncation regime non-uniform across paginated/large read surfaces — LLMs hand-wave soft truncation signals + infer completeness from partial views (lived: in-pi coder reported absent FB→FGAP edge twice based on page-1-of-128-view)
description: FGAP-135 closed the truncation-handwave class for pi's built-in `read` tool by injecting a HARD-REFUSAL directive at the pi-dispatch layer (read-truncation-gate intercepts truncated read events + replaces content with a single-text-item refusal directing the agent to paginate or grep). The same hard-refusal pattern is NOT uniformly applied across pi-context's own read surfaces. Lived evidence 2026-05-30 this session: an in-pi coding agent evaluating the spec-v2 substrate reported 'FB-018 has no FGAP edge' as a finding TWO TURNS IN A ROW. Verification: FB-018 has three matching outgoing edges in relations.json (→ project:FGAP-168 via friction_item_files_to_gap + → DEC-0009 + → CONCEPT-003); the invariant friction-item-files-to-gap-or-addressed is satisfied; no gap. Agent's own post-hoc admission (verbatim): 'I read page 1 of 128 relations edges (the first 50, ending at FB-011). FB-018's edges were on a later page I never fetched. I inferred absence from a partial view and stated it as fact.' Pattern: agent asserts → real-check disproves → agent should not have asserted without checking. Same class as FGAP-135 (pi `read` truncation handwave) but at a DIFFERENT call-site (paginated/large read surface that doesn't route through pi's `read` tool → not covered by FGAP-135's auth-gate-style interception).

evidence.reference: Returns content: [{ type: 'text', text: JSON.stringify(result, null, 2) }] — raw JSON.stringify with NO serializeForRead, NO overCapDirective, NO truncation handling. Large result gets full body OR gets outer-pi truncated with no in-band signal.

Framework HAS the truncation-refusal machinery: serializeForRead with overCapDirective produces a directive-only REFUSAL (no body) naming the appropriate paginate/grep next-call. Pattern proven in pi-context/src/index.ts at read-block (line 1296) + read-config (lines 1430 + 1445). 23 serializeForRead call-sites in index.ts; not all set overCapDirective.

Hard-refusal pattern at the pi-dispatch tool_result event handler — intercepts ONLY pi built-in `read` truncation. Does not cover pi-context's own paginated tool surfaces (read-block / context-walk-descendants / context-find-references / context-edges-for-lens / etc.).

Two-turn-repeated false claim 'FB-018 has no FGAP edge' from in-pi coding agent. Verification in main-orchestrator showed 3 satisfying edges + invariant-passing state. Agent's self-attribution to non-paginated read confirms the pattern category.

impact: Class-of-tools: any pi-context read surface returning a paginatable/large result without serializeForRead-with-overCapDirective will produce a partial-view that LLMs can hand-wave as complete. Concrete consequences: (a) in-pi agents misreport substrate state as absent when paginated past visible window (lived evidence: this session); (b) coding agents working from partial substrate make incorrect implementation decisions; (c) audit / adversarial probe agents declare CLEAN based on partial enumeration; (d) the orchestration-contract assumption that real-check disproves wrong assertions is undercut when the assertion ITSELF derives from a partial-view artifact the agent treated as complete. Compounds with the broader pattern named by FGAP-135 — pi's `read` is hard-refusal-protected but pi-context's own paginated surfaces aren't, so the in-pi LLM has uneven truncation protection across the tool surfaces it routes through.
canonical_vocabulary: truncation regime; overCapDirective uniformity; hard-refusal vs soft-truncation signal; paginated read surfaces; serializeForRead call-sites; FGAP-135 companion
proposed_resolution:
Audit all paginated / large-result returning Pi tool registrations in pi-context (and pi-agent-dispatch / pi-workflows / pi-behavior-monitors) for serializeForRead + overCapDirective usage. Apply uniformly: every Pi tool that can return a paginatable/large result MUST route through serializeForRead with an overCapDirective naming the appropriate paginate/grep next-call. Concrete sites to fix from initial survey: context-walk-descendants (line 1XXX) uses raw JSON.stringify — wrap in serializeForRead + add overCapDirective routing to paginated walk variant (or a documented offset/limit walk tool). Same survey for context-find-references / context-edges-for-lens / any other JSON.stringify-direct return. Add a tsx --test lens (test file in packages/pi-context/src/) that enumerates all Pi tool registrations + asserts each returning a list/array/large-object goes through serializeForRead. Out-of-scope alternative considered: route ALL pi-context tool outputs through a pi-dispatch-layer truncation gate (like FGAP-135 does for pi's `read`) — rejected because the per-tool overCapDirective contains tool-specific paginate hints (read-block-page vs grep vs walk-descendants offset) that the generic gate cannot produce; per-tool serializeForRead-with-overCapDirective preserves the hint specificity.

ADDITIONAL CONCRETE REQUIREMENTS (added 2026-05-30 after lived-evidence framing refined):

(R1) Element-count visibility — every truncated read MUST surface the count it returned vs the total available ("you have seen X of Y items"). Soft footers naming only byte/KB caps are insufficient because LLMs hand-wave size as opaque; explicit X/Y counts make partial-view status structurally visible. Concrete shape: every overCapDirective payload includes returned_count + total_count + truncated_at fields so the directive can render "showing 50 of 128 edges; 78 not returned" rather than "truncated at 12KB." Applies uniformly across all paginated read surfaces.

(R2) Line-number markers for file reads — pi built-in read truncation directive (FGAP-135) currently names byte/line caps; extend to ALWAYS include the precise line-range returned vs the file total line count ("returned lines 1-200 of 847") so the LLM can compute which offset to next-call without guessing. Byte-only signals leave LLMs to infer line-position from KB which they hand-wave as "probably most of it." Concrete shape: TruncationResult carries first_line + last_line + total_lines fields; buildTruncationDirective renders "returned lines X-Y of Z; next: offset=Y+1" instead of "truncated at N bytes." The four TruncationResult variant directives (lines / bytes / firstLineExceedsLimit / lastLinePartial per FGAP-135) all get the explicit line-range upgrade.

Both requirements address the same root cause as the original gap: soft truncation signals (sizes, generic warnings) get hand-waved; explicit count-of-total + line-range data force the LLM to structurally acknowledge partial-view status before composing assertions.

(R3) Monitor-class consideration — design space, not specification — pi-behavior-monitors could observe truncation encounters as a distinct activity class. Open direction: when an agent's tool_call → tool_result chain includes a truncation event AND the agent's subsequent assistant message composes assertions about the (partially-viewed) content, a monitor classifies whether the agent acknowledged partial-view or hand-waved. Hand-wave detection (the lived pattern that grounded this gap) becomes monitorable rather than relying purely on R1+R2 directive-prominence to compel structural acknowledgment. Possible monitor class: truncation-handwave-classifier. Possible signal source: pi event handlers (tool_result truncation flags + message_end content scanning). Possible steering: inject correction directive on FLAG verdict (paginate-before-asserting reminder). Specific monitor design + name + classify template + verdict shape NOT yet specified; this requirement names that monitor involvement is a load-bearing design consideration alongside the per-tool directive uniformity (R1+R2 work at the read-surface layer; R3 works at the agent-behavior-observation layer; complementary, not substitutionary).
